analogy: Think of "Structure and Physiography" through the chapter's own opening images: PHYSIOGRAPHY This unit deals with • Structure and Relief; physiographic divisions • Drainage systems: concept of water sheds — the Himalayan and the Peninsular UNIT II 2026-27 Do you know that our earth also has a history. The earth and its landforms that we see... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Structure and Physiography is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that pHYSIOGRAPHY This unit deals with • Structure and Relief; physiographic divisions • Drainage systems: concept of water sheds — the Himalayan and the Peninsular UNIT II 2026-27

The text explains that do you know that our earth also has a history.

The text explains that the earth and its landforms that we see today have evolved over a very long time.

The text explains that current estimation shows that the earth is approximately 4600 million years old.

The text explains that over these long years, it has undergone many changes brought about primarily by the endogenic and exogenic forces.

The text explains that these forces have played a significant role in giving shape to various surface and subsurface features of the earth.

The text explains that you have already studied about the Plate Tectonics and the movement of the Earth’s plates in the book Fundamentals of Physical Geography (NCERT, 2024).

The text explains that do you know that the Indian plate was to the south of the equator millions of years ago?

The text explains that do you also know that it was much larger in size and the Australian plate was a part of it?

The text explains that over millions of years, this plate broke into many parts and the Australian plate moved towards the southeastern direction and the Indian plate to the north.

The text explains that can you map different phases in the movement of the Indian plate?

The text explains that this northward movement of the Indian plate is still continuing and it has significant consequences on the physical environment of the Indian subcontinent.

The text explains that can you name some important consequences of the northward movement of the Indian plate?

The text explains that it is primarily through the interplay of these endogenic and exogenic forces and lateral movements of the plates that the present geological structure and geomorphologic processes active in the Indian subcontinent came into existence.

The text explains that based on the variations in its geological structure and formations, India can be divided into three geological divisions.

The text explains that these geological regions broadly follow the physical features: (i) The Penisular Block (ii) The Himalayas and other Peninuslar Mountains (iii) Indo-Ganga-Brahmaputra Plain.

The text explains that the south consists of stable table land with highly dissected plateaus, denuded rocks and developed series of scarps.

The text explains that the general orientation of these ranges is from northwest to the southeast direction in the northwestern part of India.

The text explains that himalayas in the Darjiling and Sikkim regions lie in an eastwest direction, while in Arunachal Pradesh they are from southwest to the northwest direction.

The text explains that in Nagaland, Manipur and Mizoram, they are in the northsouth direction.

The text explains that as a result of this, the streams and rivers coming from the mountains deposit heavy materials of rocks and boulders, and at times, disappear in this zone.

The text explains that as opposed to this, the Brahmaputra river flows from the northeast to the southwest direction before it takes an almost 90° southward turn at Dhubri before it enters into Bangladesh.

The text explains that some of the important physiographic features of this region are tors, block mountains, rift valleys, spurs, bare rocky structures, series of hummocky hills and wall-like quartzite dykes offering natural sites for water storage.

The text explains that this Peninsular plateau has undergone recurrent phases of upliftment and submergence accompanied by crustal faulting and fractures.

The text explains that this region has undergone metamorphic processes in its geological history, which can be corroborated by the presence of metamorphic rocks such as marble, slate, gneiss, etc.

The text explains that the general elevation of the Central Highlands ranges between 700-1,000 m above the mean sea level and it slopes towards the north and northeastern directions.

The text explains that an eastern extension of the Central Highland is formed by the Rajmahal hills, to the south of which lies a large reserve of mineral resources in the Chotanagpur plateau.

The text explains that similar to the Chotanagpur plateau, the Meghalaya plateau is also rich in mineral resources like coal, iron ore, sillimanite, limestone and uranium.

The text explains that cherrapunji displays a bare rocky surface devoid of any permanent vegetation cover.

The text explains that though the underlying rock structure of the desert is an extension of the Peninsular plateau, yet, due to extreme arid conditions, its surface 2026-27

The text explains that some of the well pronounced desert land features present here are mushroom rocks, shifting dunes and oasis (mostly in its southern part).

The text explains that the lakes and the playas have brackish water which is the main source of obtaining salt.

The text explains that however, some smaller islands are volcanic in origin.

The text explains that barren island, the only active volcano in India is also situated in the Andaman and Nicobar islands.

The text explains that (i) Which one of the water bodies separates the Andaman from the Nicobar?

The text explains that (a) 11°° Channel (c) 10°° Channel (b) Gulf of Mannar (d) Andaman Sea (ii) On which of the following hill range is the ‘Dodabeta’ peak situated?

The text explains that (a) Nilgiri hills (c) Cardamom hills (b) Anaimalai hills (d) Nallamala hills 2.

The text explains that (i) If a person is to travel to Lakshadweep, from which coastal plain does he prefer and why?

The text explains that (ii) Where in India will you find a cold desert?

The text explains that (iii) Why is the western coastal plain is devoid of any delta?

The text explains that (i) Make a comparison of the island groups of the Arabian Sea and the Bay of Bengal.

The text explains that (ii) What are the important geomorphological features found in the river valley plains?

The text explains that (iii) If you move from Badrinath to Sunderbans delta along the course of the river Ganga, what major geomorphological features will you come across?

The text explains that (ii) Identify the major landforms of your state and analyse the major economic activity practised by the people in each landform.

Source: NCERT Class 11 India Physical Environment, Chapter 2: Structure and Physiography

prelims_traps: Do not treat PHYSIOGRAPHY and THE PENINSULAR BLOCK as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Structure and Physiography; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Structure and Physiography through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use PHYSIOGRAPHY and THE PENINSULAR BLOCK to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
